nativeRange.habitat: Generally inhabits shallow coral reefs, rubble fields, and low intertidal zones. Tends to live in flat-spired conical snail shells (often cone shells, Conidae) that suit its flattened carapace, and is often seen wedged into reef crevices during the day before emerging to forage at night.

decorPreferences: ample live rock for grazing and shelter
sand or fine rubble substrate for foraging
a varied supply of empty cone-style shells slightly larger than the current shell
low-to-moderate flow regions where detritus accumulates
secure rockwork (large adults can topple loose frags as they climb)

diet.notes: Generally an opportunistic scavenger. Has been documented eating detritus, leftover fish food, film and filamentous algae, cyanobacteria, and small meaty items. In captivity, supplemental feedings of dried seaweed (nori), mysis shrimp, and sinking pellets are commonly advised, especially in cleaner tanks where natural detritus is limited. Iodine, calcium, and trace-element supplementation are generally suggested to support healthy molting.

goodWith: peaceful reef-safe fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, clownfish, dwarf wrasses)
other hermit crabs of similar or different size (with abundant spare shells)
ornamental shrimp (Lysmata cleaner shrimp, peppermint, fire shrimp)
established mixed reef tanks with stable rockwork
avoidWith: snails kept without a generous shell supply (the crab has been reported to kill snails for their shells)
small or expensive snails such as Trochus or Astraea in shell-limited tanks
predators large enough to crush the shell (large wrasses, triggerfish, puffers, large hawkfish)
delicate unsecured coral frags it can topple while climbing
any copper-based medication in the system

summary: Vivid orange-and-black striped marine hermit crab from Indo-Pacific reefs. Larger and more ornamental than the smaller Caribbean cleanup hermits — generally considered reef safe and beginner-friendly, but has been known to kill snails for shells and topple loose frags.
careNotes: Ciliopagurus strigatus is an Indo-Pacific reef hermit, NOT a terrestrial Coenobita land hermit — it is fully aquatic and lives only in saltwater. It is sold as a display ornamental more than a cleanup workhorse: adults reach roughly 5-8 cm (sources spread between ~5 cm and ~8 cm; the LiveAquaria purchase size of 3/4-1 1/2 inch describes juveniles), making it noticeably larger than the small Blue-leg or Scarlet hermits typical of CUC packs. The flattened carapace is an adaptation for living in flat-spired cone-shell shells (Conidae), and aquarists are generally advised to provide an assortment of cone-style empty shells in graded sizes — the species has been documented attacking and killing snails to steal their shells when suitable empties are not available. Sources broadly agree it is reef-safe but with caveats: it will not actively prey on corals, but its size and climbing habit mean it can topple unglued frags and trample flat encrusting corals while foraging. Temperament ratings split across sources (LiveAquaria 'peaceful', Reeflings 'peaceful', Aquariumbreeder 'semi-aggressive when hungry or shell-pressed') — peaceful is the typical case when shells and food are abundant. Care level is universally rated easy/beginner. Minimum tank recommendations span 15-21 US gal (~60-80 L); 75 L (≈20 US gal) is used here as a cautious round value. Native range is Indo-Pacific (Red Sea through East Africa, Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia and Australia, out to Hawaii); LiveAquaria's 'Hawaiian Islands' label reflects collection origin, not species range. Wild collection depth is typically 5-15 m on shallow reef rubble. Like all marine decapods the species is highly sensitive to copper-based fish medications and to ammonia/nitrite spikes — add only to a cycled mature tank, drip-acclimate for 1-2 hours, and treat any disease in a separate hospital tank.
breedingNotes: Generally considered impractical in captivity. Like most marine hermit crabs, the species releases planktonic zoeae that drift for an extended pelagic phase with specific salinity and food requirements that are very difficult to replicate at home — no commercial captive breeding is documented. Trade supply is entirely wild-caught.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Trade source. Provides care level (Easy), temperament (Peaceful), reef compatibility (Yes with caveats), temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, family (Diogenidae), and origin (Hawaiian Islands — reflects collection locality, not full species range).

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.2-8.4), temperature (74-78°F / 23.3-25.6°C), salinity (35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026), Indo-Pacific origin, and 'generally reef safe' rating. Confirmed cyanobacteria/algae-eating cleanup behavior.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Halloween Hermit Crab Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Provided adult size (up to 5 cm), lifespan (up to 8 years in captivity), temperature 22-28°C, pH 8.0-8.5, KH 8-16, SG 1.020-1.025, native range (Indo-West Pacific including Red Sea, India, SE Asia, Australia, Hawaii), wild depth 5-15 m, larval-rearing difficulty, and noted semi-aggressive shell-stealing behavior when empty shells are scarce.

additional.name: Reeflings — Ciliopagurus strigatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8 cm / 3 in — sets the upper bound), temperature 24-27°C, pH 8.0-8.5, SG 1.023-1.025, minimum tank 80 L (~21 US gal), family Diogenidae, order Decapoda, and Indo-Pacific range (Hawaii, Philippines, Great Barrier Reef).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically as is typical for decapods; the exact interval is not well documented for this species in hobby sources, but adult marine hermits commonly molt every few weeks to months as they grow. The crab generally retreats deeper into its shell or into a quiet rock crevice to molt and may be unseen for several days. The discarded exoskeleton is often consumed for calcium reuptake and should not be removed. Iodine supplementation is commonly advised to support successful molts. After molting the crab may seek a larger shell — keeping an assortment of graded empty cone-style shells available is generally considered important.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No commercial captive breeding documented. Releases planktonic larvae with demanding salinity and feeding requirements that are very difficult to replicate at home.
